11. PROCEDURE OF BLOCKING OF

CONTENT/WEBSITE IN INDIA

Under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, the government has
the power to issue directions for blocking public access to any information
through any computer resource. The process for blocking content and websites
generally involves the following steps:

1. Initiation: The process may be initiated by any person or entity, including
individuals, organizations, or government authorities. If someone believes that
certain content or websites are objectionable, harmful, or violate the laws of
the country, they can approach the appropriate authority to request a block. 2. Complaint or representation: The concerned authority, such as the Ministry
of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), or designated officer, should
be approached with a written complaint or representation. The complaint should
provide details of the objectionable content or website and reasons for blocking.

3. Examination and assessment: The authority will examine the complaint
and assess whether it falls within the purview of objectionable or unlawful
content as per the provisions of the Information Technology Act. They may also
seek clarification or additional information if required.

4. Show cause notice: If the authority finds the complaint valid, they will
issue a show-cause notice to the content provider or the website owner, providing
them an opportunity to present their case and explain their position. The notice
will also inform them of the intention to block the content or website. 5. Hearing and representation: The content provider or website owner can
file a representation explaining their stance and challenging the blocking order.
They may present arguments, evidence, or legal provisions supporting their
case. This may involve hearings or written submissions. The authority will
consider these representations before making a final decision.

6. Blocking order: If the authority is satisfied that the objectionable content
or website violates the law, they may issue a blocking order. This order will
direct internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to the content or website
across India. The blocking order should specify the reasons and legal provisions
under which it is issued.

7. Compliance: ISPs are responsible for implementing the blocking order
and ensuring that the content or website is inaccessible to users in India. They
may employ technical means to achieve this, such as DNS blocking or IP
address-based blocking.

12. MLAT (MUTUAL LEGAL AGREEMENT TREATY) and

LR (LETTERS REGULATORY)

Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty (MLAT) is an agreement between two or
more countries that facilitates cooperation between their law enforcement
agencies in investigating and prosecuting criminal offenses. It is a tool used to
assist in obtaining evidence, collecting information, and conducting
investigations in cross-border cases.

MLATs are particularly relevant in cases involving cyber crimes, as crimes
committed online often transcend national borders. They allow countries to
exchange information, request assistance in gathering evidence, and cooperate
in the extradition of offenders.

India has signed MLATs with several countries, including the United States,
the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and many others. These treaties provide
a legal framework for cooperation in criminal matters, including cyber crimes. Under an MLAT, requests for assistance are made through the respective
central authorities of the countries involved. The central authority in the
requesting country submits a request to the central authority in the requested
country, specifying the nature of the assistance required. The requested country
then evaluates the request and carries out the necessary actions within its
legal framework.

MLATs typically cover various aspects of cooperation, such as the exchange
of information, gathering evidence, locating and identifying suspects, freezing
and forfeiting assets, and facilitating the transfer of convicted persons.

The laws of all countries are territorial in nature; they are applicable
indomesticboundaries ofcountries inwhichtheyarelegislated.

may beavailable in a country or place outside India, any Criminal Court may
issue a letterof request to a Court or an authority in that country or place
competent to deal withsuch request to examine orally any person supposed to
be acquainted with the factsand circumstances of the case and to record his
statement made in the course ofsuch examination and also to require such
person or any other person to produce any document or thing which may be in
his possession pertaining to the case and to forward all the evidence so taken
or collected or the authenticated copies thereof or the thing so collected to the

Court issuing such letter. PROCEDURE OF BLOCKING OF CONTENT / WEBSITE IN INDIA

The International Police Cooperation Cell (here in after referred as IPCC) of
CBI, New Delhi is the designated agency for routing requests for in formal enquiries
to be made with National Central Bureau of other countries, Interpol
Headquarters as well as our Missionsabroad.

For getting informal investigation conducted through the Interpol
channelsor our Missions abroad, a self contained request, along with necessary
details, may be addressed to Assistant Director, IPCC, Block # 4, CGO Complex,
Central Bureau of Investigation, New Delhi. In case, information is to be
collected/ enquiries are to be conducted in more than one country, separate
self contained requests may be sent for each country. The request may be
routed through the head of Crime Investigation Department (CID) of the State
Police.

The request must in corporate the following details: The request must in corporate the following details:

The FIR number along with names of the accused and sections of law
under which case has been registered.

The gist of the allegations in the FIR/Preliminary Enquiry or any other
Investigation Process.

The detail of information required. In order to facilitate requestedcountry/
its NCB providing information the specific relevant detailsmust be furnished.

It is necessary that material being furnished should be carefully examined
and scrutinised at an appropriate level especially as regards accuracy of factsand
figures. It must be noted the information so collected cannot be treatedas
formalevidence.

VISIT OF POLICE OFFICERS ABROAD FOR INVESTIGATION VISIT OF POLICE OFFICERS ABROAD FOR INVESTIGATION

Sometimes,itmaybecomenecessaryto send Police Officer(s) from India to
foreign country for the purpose of execution of LR or for collecting information
or leads during the course of investigation of a case keeping inview the
importance of the case and the complicated nature of offences under
investigation.

As any police officer including that of the CBI would enjoy no Policepowers
in a foreign country and any such visit by a police officer withouttheexpress
consent of any country may be considered interference in the sovereignty of
that country unless some required formalities are observed.

Whenitisconsidered necessary to send a team of Officers abroad, the State
Government will send a proposal to IPCC, CBI, India which in turn will obtain
the approval of MHA for the proposed visit, whenever necessary. PROCEDURE OF BLOCKING OF CONTENT / WEBSITE IN INDIA

The following information needs to be sent to the IPCC, CBI India for
taking up the matter with the country to which such team is proposed to besent:

A brief note detailing the reasons for sending the team, nature ofenquiries
required to be made in the requested country. This is to enable the authorities
to assess whether the request is justified.

All available particulars about identity or particulars of the person tobe
contacted or documents to be scrutinised etc. This would help the requested
country to make all necessary preparations.

Information about penal offence to which mission relates.

Whether Article 3 of the ICPO (Interpol) Constitution or some other legal
provision restricting international cooperation is attracted.

Exact date and duration of the mission and information about the police
officers such as their names andranks.

Any other relevant information which may be relevant in processing such
arequest. Any other relevant information which may be relevant in processing such
arequest.

The visit will not commence before the required permission is received.
The officers must get in touch with Indian Mission on their arrival. In case, the
country does not have a mission, the accredited mission for India may be kept
informed as regards visit of the officers.

It is important to note that MLATs have certain limitations and challenges.
The process can be time-consuming due to the involvement of multiple
authorities and the need for translations and legal requirements. Moreover,
some countries may have specific limitations on the types of assistance they
can provide or conditions they may impose.

ICS OF BLOCK CHAIN AND CRYPTO

1. What is Blockchain
2. Crypto Exchanges ( Domestic and International)
3. Crypto Trading ( B2B,P2P, B2P)

Blockchain is a distributed and decentralized technology that serves as the
underlying foundation for cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and many other applications.
It is essentially a chain of blocks, where each block contains a list of transactions.
These blocks are linked together using cryptographic principles, creating a secure
and immutable ledger of all the transactions ever made on the network.

Key characteristics of block chain:

Decentralization: Instead of relying on a central authority, blockchain is
distributed across a network of computers (nodes) that work together to validate and
record transactions.

Transparency: All transactions on the blockchain are visible to anyone with
access to the network, promoting transparency and trust. Transparency: All transactions on the blockchain are visible to anyone with
access to the network, promoting transparency and trust.

Immutability: Once a block is added to the blockchain, it cannot be altered or
deleted, ensuring the integrity of the data.

Security: Cryptography is used to secure transactions and control the creation
of new blocks, making the blockchain resistant to tampering and fraud.

Blockchain technology has applications beyond cryptocurrencies, such as supply
chain management, voting systems, smart contracts, and more.

Crypto Exchanges (Domestic and International):

Crypto exchanges are platforms that facilitate the buying, selling, and trading
of cryptocurrencies. They act as intermediaries, allowing users to exchange their fiat
currency (like USD, EUR, etc.) or other cryptocurrencies for various cryptocurrencies
and vice versa.

a. Domestic Crypto Exchanges: a. Domestic Crypto Exchanges:

Domestic crypto exchanges are those that operate within a specific country’s
borders, catering to users within that country. These exchanges typically support
local fiat currencies and comply with the regulations of that country.

b. International Crypto Exchanges:

International crypto exchanges are platforms that operate across multiple
countries and serve users from different parts of the world. They often support a wide
range of cryptocurrencies and fiat currency pairs.

It’s important to note that regulations and services offered by exchanges can vary
greatly depending on the country they operate in. Some countries have more stringent
regulations, while others have more lenient or undefined frameworks for
cryptocurrencies. BASICS OF BLOCK CHAIN AND CRYPTO

Crypto Trading (B2B, P2P, B2P):
a. B2B (Business-to-Business) Crypto Trading:

B2B crypto trading refers to the exchange of cryptocurrencies between
businesses. This can involve large transactions, such as institutional investors or
companies buying and selling cryptocurrencies for investment or other purposes.

b. P2P (Peer-to-Peer) Crypto Trading:

P2P crypto trading involves direct transactions between individuals without the
need for an intermediary like an exchange. P2P platforms connect buyers and sellers,
allowing them to negotiate prices and conduct trades directly. P2P trading is often
used in countries with limited access to traditional banking systems or in cases
where individuals prefer more privacy and control over their transactions.

c. B2P (Business-to-Peer) Crypto Trading: c. B2P (Business-to-Peer) Crypto Trading:

B2P crypto trading is the exchange of cryptocurrencies between businesses
and individual users. It often involves businesses offering goods or services and
accepting cryptocurrencies as payment from individual customers.

on Map based on location of their mobile phones at the time of offence.

the target calls are initiated based on location of mobiles. 5. Generating alerts on near real time basis to the states, from where

6. To establish Crime Links and Criminal Networks within and across
the states through call data analysis (Direct and indirect links)

criminal based on his phone, IMEI or call data links.

Executive Summary:

The role of Police in protecting peace and public order in any society cannot
be overemphasized and it is even more critical in the current day scenario of
technology disruption.

Telangana Police, in its ceaseless Endeavour to bring in development in
Policing services, has introduced an intervention of restructuring its core police
functions into different Functional Verticals (FV) with an intention to utilize
people talent and potential to optimal levels.

This document delineates the Why, What and How of Functional Verticals
structure introduced in Telangana Police in 2016 and discusses the significance,
critical success factors and the results of this game changing intervention.

FUNCTIONAL VERTICALS STRUCTURE IN TELANGANA STATE POLICE
A Best Practice Report
Introduction: FUNCTIONAL VERTICALS STRUCTURE IN TELANGANA STATE POLICE
A Best Practice Report
Introduction:

Since ages and time immemorial, individuals and societies on the globe
have been relentlessly striving to have better placed and suitable policing services
in some or other form to cater to their security concerns and for their overall
wellbeing. Aftermath of several revolutions in economic, social, political and
cultural spheres of humankind, policing had to redefine and modify the services
and systems.

Starting from Sir Robert Peel, the father of modern policing to Sardar
Vallabhbhai Patel, the first Home Minister of independent India, history is the
witness to the transformation and evolution of police as an organization for
ensuring the safety and security of people. In the current times, there is even greater need to bring in professionalism
at the cutting-edge level through a citizen and community-centric responsive
and service delivery organization, well defined roles and responsibilities of staff,
embracing latest technologies, high utilization of IT
and competencies through customized trainings in various areas to combat
the challenges.

interventions, build skills

Given the nature of police work, the SHO, gets overwhelmed every day in
firefighting exercise. While being so, he allocates all and sundry tasks
mechanically on a daily basis, to the available personnel without following any
established principles of work management, including division of work,

functional specialization, team culture, measuring the individual output, morale IMPLEMENTATION OF 17 FUNCTIONAL VERTICALS IN TELANGANA POLICE

and motivation etc. We have not put in place, any established systems for
promoting efficiency at the police station level, except a set of instructions for
Station House Management laid down in the police manuals.

As a result, the work culture at the police station remained command-
driven without any initiative and ownership among the ranks, leading to
stagnation of quality of service delivery at police station. No concerted effort
has ever been made by the police leadership so far, to identify and implement
necessary systems and best practices aimed at improving the productivity of
individual employee at the police station level.

The challenge before the leadership therefore, is to ensure that each of the
police personnel deployed at the police station is proactive and delivers his
optimal performance on a daily basis by appropriate interventions.

Need for Functional Verticals in Police: Need for Functional Verticals in Police:

Police is among the government departments having largest manpower,
requiring the leadership focus on manpower productivity, with a view to get
optimal output from each and every employee of the organization.

Telangana State Police has a sanctioned strength of 80,000 persons across
all levels. For ages, the Police department worked in hierarchical and
conventional ways prior to the change initiatives introduced in 2016 in TS
Police. Earlier, each police station in metropolitan cities has employees easily
from 50 to 150 in a Police station and is headed by the Station House Officer
(SHO). A SHO is required to manage all 150 employees single handed as there
is no defined work structure and assigned roles to these employees. On an ad-
hoc basis, employees are assigned various duties based on sole decision of
SHO who may be busy in his /her responsibilities leaving very little scope for
people management in professional manner. While the internal culture in Police was not agile to suit the ever-growing
demands of the public, grim crime scenario including cybercrime always

challenged Police services in big way.

The State expects that the Police department is vital in keeping the state
in safe and secure manner with high public order.

Ensuring peace and harmony in the state is the key to its economic
development

Crime and insurgency has always been a great challenge in states like
Telangana while technology related cybercrimes try to outsmart police
interventions

Unless, committed and accountable workforce produce optimal results in

all functions they do, round the clock, in all places they work - meeting these
expectations will be a great challenge. Current hierarchical structure and culture
within Police department makes it nearly impossible to meet these expectations
from different stakeholders in the State. Thus, there were multiple issues that
Police has to deal with:

Key challenges confronted by Telangana Police:

° How to make leaders and employees contribute to public safety and security
ensuring peace and public order?

° How to deliver uniform service across time and place without prejudice to
fear or favor?

° How to transition from person-centric management style to system-centric
management style?

° How to extract optimal productivity from all employees?
° How to get ownership from every employee?
° How to motivate employees transparently based on performance? To find solutions to these critical concerns, Telangana State Police
envisioned a planned strategy to initiate Functional Verticals structure with
matrix reporting system to meet its Mission and Vision:

This meant there was a need for a complete rethink, redesign and revamp
of the way work is done at the Police Station level.

The Concept of Functional Verticals - a Game Changer The Concept of Functional Verticals - a Game Changer

Telangana State Police has initiated the division of work at police station
into 17 Vertical Functions as the efficient service delivery units to serve better
the citizens and communities. The division of work will serve as actively seeking
to build trust, with the citizens and communities, clarity on roles &
responsibilities will create ownership and responsiveness among the staff,
healthy work culture and professionalism, increase in transparency &
accountability, improve productivity and efficiency, sustain morale and
motivation through Reward & Recognition system, and it helps in assimilation IMPLEMENTATION OF 17 FUNCTIONAL VERTICALS IN TELANGANA POLICE

and dissemination of information. This will help in keeping up the morale and
motivation levels of the entire staff at all Police Stations on a regular basis for
improving the productivity of each individual employee and the Police
organization at large. Traditionally Police departments across the country are designed on
hierarchy model, where every employee reports to a single superior officer who
controls and commands staff -— highly dependent on the superior’s competence
to lead. FV structure enables an employee to report to two superiors — one the
functional vertical head and the other the work unit head (SHO / Unit officer).
Functional Vertical units define and standardize every role, train, monitor and
reward performance. Work unit Officer supervises the service delivery. Thus,
the employee becomes a functional vertical expert delivering duties in an
assigned work unit. Monitoring of functional verticals is done at all levels, all
wings and across geographical locations. Hierarchical structure differed and
limited in significant ways compared to the Functional Verticals structure:

Functional Verticals -Design and Structure

The Functional Verticals in every Police Station to work, the roles of each
employee at different levels were defined and assigned to one of these 17
functions. They are given tasks only related to their respective functions, except
in occasional cases of Bandobust. The intended objective of this design is to
develop competent professionals in the function who are trained, equipped
and motivated to provide the best service in the roles assigned to them. In
other words, each employee becomes an organizational change champion. Operational structure of FVs:

The Structure of the Vertical Functions within the current organization
structure is enabled in the below structure. It is a three-dimensional matrix
structure where:

Vertical Function is defined as nodal function at an organization level and
is being managed by Vertical Function Champion in the rank of Supervisory
officers;

Unit Function is defined at City (Zone-level) / District level and is being
managed by Unit Champion

Station House is a regular local management function at the police station
unit level still holds delivery of citizen services apart from aspects of general
duty responsibilities. The head is being known as Station House Officer (SHO).

FV Centre of Excellence (CoE) at State level :

The Mission of Centre of Excellence at the State Headquarters is to ensure
uniform service delivery across the State at any time at any place to any citizen.
Hence, the role and purpose of CoE is to be the Key Driver of organizational
Change in all functional verticals.

FCC Set-up at Unit/District level : FCC Set-up at Unit/District level :

The Functional Vertical set-up at the Unit (City/Commissionerate) / District
levels acts as support structure to the State level Centre of Excellence (CoE).
Each Unit/ District is evolved as the Functional Competency Centre (FCC) and
act as a link-pin between the Police stations and the State level CoE for seamless
coordination. FCCs are the medium through which CoE makes up for its
knowledge and skill deficit ensuring homogeneity of functional services across
the state. To enable this mechanism effectively, FV employees at the police
station report to their respective functional head at the Unit FCC and at the
same time to the administrative head (SHO) at the Police station, thus facilitating
Matrix structure in the organization.

FV Teams at Police Station level : FV Teams at Police Station level :

The Functional verticals at the cutting edge level act as agile teams, who
are trained, equipped and empowered to take routine decisions on day-to-day
issues thereby reducing the dependency on the SHO except in critical cases.
As the FV Team members are specialized and possess expertise in their relevant
roles the quality of service delivery is assured.

Thus, the structure operates in the following manner:

Role and functions of Centre of Excellence (CoE):

Centre of Excellence is the key change driver in this restructuring exercise.
The Thought Leadership as a think-tank reimagined the ideal ways of Police
working and designed this transformational intervention. CoE is expected to
be the driver of the intervention and operates like a control unit in ensuring
functional verticals are smoothly working. The key roles and responsibilities of
CoE are:

Define the FV roles in all levels

Design Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)

Determine Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

Impart Training to FV teams vertical-wise

Provide IT resources, tools and infrastructure

Track and measure Performance against KPIs and
feedback

Reward and Recognize Best Performance at all levels

Critical Success Factors of Functional Vertical structure:

Every Police Station in Telangana State operates in FV structure where
every employee is pre-assigned permanent roles with exceptions of emergencies.
The unique features of this structure are:

Organization Change Transition Process through Paradigm shift

Organization Change process is always a challenge in most organization
and more so in the case of hierarchy driven Police organization where sub-
culture strongly determines the way of people functioning. Attempting change
transition requires a mission-mode commitment, and drive from the top
management and relentless efforts over a long drawn period of time while facing
and tackling resistance from the system, lack of time, priority and resources
and most importantly lack of talent within the organization to drive change.
Any typical organizational change model goes through the following
progressions:

Telangana Police initiative to bring functional verticals within their
structure was a process that was initiated in the year 2016 and phase-wise
extended to other functional departments like Traffic, Armed Reserve etc. The
process of this massive change initiative of transitioning to new ways of working
was facilitated by the processes of:

Key driver to the FV initiative was the Mission and Vision of the State
Police with one key objective -Uniform Service Delivery across the state and
Stakeholder satisfaction against their expectations

Extensive discussions with various stakeholders to gain buy-in from various
quarters

Developing of strategies for mitigating risks such as going with the pace of
change without being imposed or forced upon, piloting with some units and
scaling up Addressing initial apprehension of employees about the inherent purpose
and transparency in the new structure

Assuaging fears of executives in the leadership (SHOs to Senior officers)
in terms of scope of power and role erosion

Anticipating and fixing potential conflict zones in terms of power-sharing
by using transparent and technology driven mechanisms to reduce human
interference

The Internet of Things (IoT) is a network of physical objects that are
embedded with sensors, software, and other technologies to collect and exchange
data. These objects are connected to the internet and can be controlled remotely.

Here are some examples of IoT devices:

Smart homes: Smart homes use IoT devices to control appliances, lights,
and security systems. For example, you can use your smartphone to turn on
the lights or lock the door when you’re not home.

Wearables: Wearable devices, such as smartwatches and fitness trackers,
collect data about your health and activity. This data can be used to track your
progress, improve your health, or even diagnose medical conditions.

Industrial IoT: Industrial IoT (IIoT) uses IoT devices to monitor and control
industrial equipment. This can help to improve efficiency, safety, and
productivity in manufacturing plants. Transportation: IoT devices are used in transportation to track vehicles,
monitor traffic, and improve safety. For example, self-driving cars use IoT devices
to navigate the road and avoid obstacles.

Agriculture: IoT devices are used in agriculture to monitor crops, livestock,
and irrigation systems. This can help to improve crop yields, reduce waste,
and save water.

These are just a few examples of the many IoT devices and applications
that are available today. As IoT technology continues to develop, we can expect
to see even more innovative and groundbreaking applications in the future.

Here are some additional examples of IoT applications:

Smart cities: Smart cities use IoT devices to collect data about traffic,
pollution, and other urban factors. This data can be used to improve city
planning, manage resources, and make cities more livable. Healthcare: IoT devices are used in healthcare to monitor patients, track
medical equipment, and deliver medication. This can help to improve patient
care and reduce costs.

Retail: IoT devices are used in retail to track inventory, monitor customer
behavior, and provide personalized recommendations. This can help retailers
to improve customer satisfaction and increase sales.

The IoT is a rapidly growing field with a wide range of potential applications.
As the technology continues to develop, we can expect to see even more
innovative and ground-breaking applications in the future.. LATEST TECHNOLOGIES

17. OSINT (OPEN SOURCE INTELLIGENCE

TECHNIQUES)

OSINT, or Open Source Intelligence, is the process of collecting and analyzing
information from publicly available sources to gather insights and knowledge about
a particular subject or target. The techniques you mentioned (Websites, BOTS, and
Link Grabbing Techniques) are relevant in the context of OSINT. Let’s explore each of

Open Source Data (OSD)

Open Source Information (OSI)

1) Open Source Data (OSD). Data is the raw print, broadcast, oral debriefing,
or other form of information from a primary source. It can be a photograph, a
tape recording, a commercial satellite image, or a personal letter from an
individual. 2) Open Source Information (OSI). OSI comprises the raw data that can be
put together, generally by an editorial process that provides some filtering and
validation and presentation management. OSI is generic information that is
usually widely disseminated. Newspapers, books, broadcasts, and general daily
reports are part of the OSI world. An example is a reporter who takes the raw
data and converts it into a meaningful article that is printed in newspapers,
magazines or broadcasted over the airwaves. 3) Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT). OSINT is information that has been
deliberately discovered, discriminated, distilled, and disseminated to a select
audience, generally the commander and his/her immediate staff, in order to
address a specific question. In the case of a battlefield commander, it would
more than likely be answering the priority intelligence requirements (PIR) or
specific orders or requests (SOR). OSINT, in other words, applies the proven
process of intelligence to the broad diversity of open sources of information,
and creates intelligence.

4) Validated OSINT (OSINT-V). OSINT-V is information to which a very high

degree of certainty can be attributed. It can only be produced by an all-source
intelligence professional, with access to classified intelligence sources, whether OSINT

working for a nation or for a coalition staff. It can also come from an assured

open source to which no question can be raised concerning its validity (

video of an aircraft arriving at an airport that is broadcast over the media).

Websites:

Websites are one of the primary sources for OSINT. By using search engines,
online databases, and other tools, you can extract valuable information from
websites. Some useful OSINT techniques related to websites include:

a. Search Engine Queries: Use advanced search operators to narrow down
your search and find specific information from search engines like Google,
Bing, or Yandex.

b. Website Enumeration: Use tools like “subdomain enumeration” to
discover subdomains related to a target domain. Tools like “theHarvester” can
help with this. c. Archive.org: Check the Wayback Machine on archive.org to access older
versions of a website, which might contain historical information.

d. Whois Lookup: Use Whois databases to find registration details of a
domain, including the domain owner’s contact information.

e. Website Crawling: Crawling tools like “Scrapy” or “wget” can help extract
data from websites in a structured manner.

BOTS (Bots on Social Media Platforms):

Social media platforms are a rich source of information, and OSINT can
be performed by utilizing bots or automated scripts to gather data from these
platforms. It’s important to note that using bots should comply with the terms

of service of the respective platforms. Some techniques related to bots in OSINT
include: OSINT

a. Twitter Bots: Create Twitter bots to monitor and collect tweets containing
specific keywords, hashtags, or mentions related to your target.

b. Reddit Bots: Develop Reddit bots to track discussions, comments, or
submissions about a particular topic or user.

c. Instagram Bots: Bots can be used to scrape data from Instagram profiles
or track posts with certain hashtags.

Link Grabbing Techniques:

Link grabbing techniques are used to discover and extract links associated
with a target or subject. These links might lead to hidden information, related
websites, or affiliated pages. Some techniques to grab links in OSINT include:

a. Google Dorks: Use specific search strings to find directories or documents
that may not be directly indexed by search engines.

b. Link Scraping Tools: Utilize web scraping tools to extract links from web
pages and documents. b. Link Scraping Tools: Utilize web scraping tools to extract links from web
pages and documents.

c. Link Analysis Tools: Use tools like “Maltego” to visualize and explore
relationships between different links, domains, and entities.

d. Social Engineering: Use social engineering techniques to trick individuals
into sharing URLs or revealing hidden links.

and China. The era of modern computers, however, began with the analytical
engine of Charles Babbage. In 1820, Joseph-Marie Jacquard, a textile
manufacturer in France, produced the loom. This device allowed the repetition
of a series of steps in the weaving of special fabrics. This resulted in a fear
amongst Jacquard’s employees that their traditional employment and livelihood
were being threatened. They committed acts of sabotage to discourage Jacquard
from further use of the new technology. This is the first recorded cybercrime!

He was finally arrested on Heathrow airport on his way to Switzerland The Information Technology Act, 2000 (IT Act, 2000) and the Information
Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008 (IT Act, 2008) are laws formulated in India
to regulate and govern electronic transactions, digital signatures, cybercrimes,
and cyber security. These acts aim to provide legal recognition to electronic
records, digital signatures, and electronic transactions.

Law enforcement officers play a crucial role in implementing and enforcing
these laws. They are responsible for investigating and addressing cybercrime-
related offenses. Both the IT Act, 2000 and IT Act, 2008 provide provisions and
powers to law enforcement officers to carry out their duties effectively.

Jurisdiction of courts: Jurisdiction of courts:

The IT Act, 2000 and IT Act, 2008 are applicable throughout India. The
jurisdiction of the courts for offenses under these acts depends on the nature
and severity of the offense. Generally, magistrates’ courts and session’s courts
have jurisdiction to handle cases arising out of these acts. For more serious
offenses, the matter may be transferred to higher courts.

In simple terms, these acts enable law enforcement officers to: INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY ACT 2000 & 2008

Investigate and prevent cybercrimes: The acts empower law enforcement
officers to investigate and prevent offenses such as hacking, cyber fraud,
identity theft, and unauthorized access to computer systems. They can
initiate and conduct investigations related to cybercrimes.

Seize electronic evidence: Law enforcement officers have the authority to
seize and retain computer systems, data, and other electronic evidence
related to cybercrimes.

Secure assistance from service providers: The acts allow law enforcement
officers to seek assistance from service providers, such as internet service
providers and intermediaries, to facilitate the investigation and gather
evidence.

Issue warrants and conduct searches: Law enforcement officers may obtain
warrants to search and seize computer systems or electronic devices
suspected to be involved in cybercrimes. They can also carry out searches
and seizures as per the provisions of the acts. Prosecute offenders: These acts provide provisions for the prosecution of
individuals involved in cybercrimes. Law enforcement officers can file
charge sheets and present evidence to courts to ensure the prosecution of
offenders.

The IT Act, 2000 is the primary law governing electronic transactions,

digital signatures, cybercrimes, and cybersecurity. It contains various sections
dealing with different aspects of information technology and its legal framework.
Some of the important sections of the IT Act, 2000 are:

computer resources and communication devices.

Section 66C: Criminalizes identity theft and impersonation using electronic
means.

Section 66D: Covers punishment for cheating by impersonation by using
